She is organising a dinner to raise money for charity and is hoping for at least 150 people. The evening charge from six pm to midnight is £115. There is also a returnable deposit of £200, which must be paid in cash. Parking is included. She will need to pay extra for the kitchen — £25. She must get a licence if she plans to have a band. About ten days before the event, she needs to contact the caretaker Mr Brown to arrange entry.

4.scene: 商业人类学研究方法讲座

signpost.quote: Let's look at some examples of how ethnographic research works in business.
opts: Shifting from definition to concrete case studies

signpost.quote: So what makes studies like these different from ordinary research?
opts: Signalling a move from specific examples to general principles

signpost.quote: But that doesn't mean that the researcher never talks to the participants.
opts: Qualifying a previous statement to avoid misunderstanding

signpost.quote: However, unlike in traditional research, in this case it's the participant rather than the researchers who decides what direction the interview will follow.
opts: Highlighting a key methodological difference between ethnography and conventional research

signpost.quote: Most researchers estimate that 70 to 80 per cent of their time is spent not on the collecting of data but on its analysis.
opts: Emphasising the importance of analysis as the dominant part of the research process

Phone company designed phones globally with a built-in call-time

mindmap.title: Key Principles of Ethnography
items: talking about themselves and being observed
in the data, not on sensational exceptions

dictation.speaker: LECTURER
parts: Ethnography is a type of research aimed at exploring the way human
work. It can offer businesses deeper insight into the needs of customers and help companies design new products or services that customers really want.

One team did a project for a company manufacturing kitchen equipment. They watched cooks use measuring cups — the numbers were inside but the cooks couldn't see them easily. So a new design of cup was developed, and it was a top
Another team looked at how cell phones were used in Uganda. People who didn't have their own phones could pay to use
phones. These customers were eager to know how much time they'd spent on the call. So phones were designed globally with this added

Ethnographic research in one computer company found that IT administrators spent a large amount of time communicating with colleagues to solve problems, but had no standard way of exchanging information from
. This led to an idea for software to help them.
Researchers who observed nurses found they needed to access the computer
of patients no matter where they were. This led to the development of a portable computer
What makes ethnography different? First, the researcher has to be completely
. They haven't thought up a hypothesis to test — they wait for the
to inform them.
Participants must be comfortable talking about themselves and being
. Most researchers say people open up pretty easily, often because they're in their own home or workplace.
Unlike in traditional research, in ethnography it is the participant rather than the researcher who decides what direction the
will follow.
Most researchers estimate that 70 to 80 per cent of their time is spent on
— looking at photos, listening to recordings and transcribing them. They try to identify a
in the data to discern the meaning behind it.

Where does the duck race start?

opts: A free duck for the next race

1.errorText: Melanie phones the Stretton Festival box office. Rob tells her there are still tickets available for the jazz band at £12. The venue is the secondary school. An additional performer, Carolyn Hart, will play the oboe with the band. For the duck races, you buy a yellow plastic duck for £1 each at a stall in the park. The ducks are launched from behind the library and float 500 metres to the railway bridge. Winners get free tickets for the flower show. Admission to the flower show is free and it is held at Bythwaite Hall, the oldest building in town.

4.scene: 土壤碳储存与气候变化讲座

signpost.quote: Rattan Lal first came to the idea that soil might be valuable not through an interest in climate change, but rather out of concern for the land itself.
opts: Explaining the unexpected origin of a key insight before developing the scientific argument

signpost.quote: Let me explain. For millions of years, carbon dioxide levels have been regulated in part by a natural partnership between plants and microbes.
opts: Signalling a more detailed mechanistic explanation of a process just introduced

signpost.quote: The invention of agriculture some 10,000 years ago disrupted these ancient soil-building processes.
opts: Introducing the turning point where human activity began to affect the natural carbon cycle

signpost.quote: Researchers are now coming up with evidence that even modest changes to farming can significantly help.
opts: Introducing a solution-oriented shift after establishing the problem

signpost.quote: Rattan Lal argues that farmers should receive payment not just for the corn or beef they produce, but also for the carbon they can store in their soil.
opts: Proposing a policy mechanism to incentivise carbon storage as part of a forward-looking conclusion

dictation.speaker: LECTURER
parts: Rattan Lal claims the world's agricultural soils could potentially absorb 13 per cent of the carbon dioxide in the atmosphere — the equivalent of the amount released in the last
years.
Carbon-rich soil is dark, crumbly and fertile and retains some water. But if soil contains inadequate amounts of carbon it becomes
and prone to erosion.
In the 1970s and '80s, Lal was studying soils in Africa so devoid of organic matter that the ground had become extremely
, like cement.
For millions of years, CO₂ levels have been regulated by a natural partnership between plants and microbes. Plants absorb CO₂ and transform it into
and other carbon substances.
Some carbon transfers from plant
to fungi and soil microbes, which store the carbon in the soil.
The invention of agriculture 10,000 years ago disrupted these processes. When humans ploughed topsoil, they exposed buried carbon to
. This created CO₂ and released it into the air.
Researchers now have evidence that even modest changes to farming can significantly help. Some growers are using 'regenerative agriculture' — keeping fields planted all year round and increasing the
of plants grown.
In California, Whendee Silver is testing the effects on carbon storage of compost created from agricultural waste and
waste.
In Australia, Christine Jones and 12 farmers are building up soil carbon by cultivating
that stay green all year round.
Rattan Lal argues that farmers should receive
not just for the food they produce, but also for the carbon they can store in their soil.
